bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — file-based routing as architecture, route-level colocation of loader/error/loading
bytes.title: Next.js App Router — File-Based Routing as Architecture
bytes.body:
The App Router doesn't just map URLs to components — it enforces a colocation model where each route segment owns its full request lifecycle. Drop a `loading.tsx` beside your `page.tsx` and Next.js automatically wraps it in a `<Suspense>` boundary. Add `error.tsx` and you get an `ErrorBoundary` scoped to that segment — parent segments remain interactive when a child throws. `layout.tsx` persists across navigations without remounting, making it the right place for context providers and persistent UI like sidebars.

This means your folder structure *is* your architecture. A `app/dashboard/settings/` directory implicitly defines a nested layout tree, a scoped loading shell, and an isolated error surface. Route groups (`(group)/`) let you share layouts without polluting the URL. Parallel routes (`@slot/`) let two independent segments render in the same layout. When you think in segments instead of pages, feature boundaries map 1:1 to directories — making code-splitting, error isolation, and streaming feel like natural byproducts of how you organize files.
bytes.code_snippet:
app/
  dashboard/
    layout.tsx          // persists on sub-navigation
    loading.tsx         // Suspense boundary — streams immediately
    error.tsx           // ErrorBoundary scoped to /dashboard/**
    page.tsx            // default export → /dashboard
    settings/
      page.tsx          // /dashboard/settings
      loading.tsx       // independent Suspense shell
    (overview)/
      analytics/
        page.tsx        // /dashboard/analytics — shares dashboard layout

bytes.topic: Component Design — Server Components vs Client Components, push use client to the leaves
bytes.title: Next.js Server vs Client Components — Push `use client` to the Leaves
bytes.body:
By default, every component in the Next.js App Router is a Server Component — it renders on the server, has zero JS bundle impact, and can directly `await` database calls or read environment secrets. The mistake developers make is hoisting `'use client'` too high, converting entire subtrees to client components unnecessarily.

The pattern: keep layout shells, data-fetching wrappers, and static UI as Server Components. Only push `'use client'` down to the specific leaf that needs `useState`, `useEffect`, or browser APIs. A `<ProductPage>` that fetches data stays a Server Component — only its `<AddToCartButton>` child needs the directive.

This matters because `'use client'` marks a boundary: everything below it — including its imports — ships to the browser. A single misplaced directive on a parent can pull in heavy dependencies client-side that should never leave the server.

Use Next.js's `next/dynamic` with `{ ssr: false }` for third-party components that access `window` at import time, keeping the boundary explicit and intentional.

bytes.topic: State Management — URL as state for filters/pagination, server state in loaders not useState
bytes.title: Next.js State Management — URL as Truth for Filters and Pagination
bytes.body:
Storing filters and pagination in `useState` is a common mistake in Next.js apps. It breaks deep-linking, loses state on refresh, and forces client-side fetching when the App Router already gives you a better primitive: the URL.

In the App Router, `searchParams` flows into every Server Component as a prop. A page like `/products?category=tools&page=2` gives you `{ category: 'tools', page: '2' }` at the server level — no `useEffect`, no loading spinner, no client bundle overhead. The data fetch happens in the Server Component using those params directly.

For client-side filter interactions, use `useRouter` and `useSearchParams` from `next/navigation`. Push new params with `router.push` or `router.replace` — the Server Component re-renders automatically with the updated URL. This makes every filter state shareable, bookmarkable, and crawlable by default.

Avoid `useState` for anything the server can own. If a value drives a data fetch, it belongs in the URL. Reserve `useState` for purely ephemeral UI state: open/close modals, hover states, uncontrolled input buffers.

bytes.topic: Performance — SSG vs SSR vs ISR decision matrix, parallel data fetching in loaders
bytes.title: Next.js Rendering Modes — SSG vs SSR vs ISR Decision Matrix + Parallel Data Fetching
bytes.body: Pick the wrong rendering mode and you pay in either stale data or cold-start latency. The rule: default to SSG (`generateStaticParams` + no `dynamic` export), upgrade only when you need it. Use SSR (`export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'`) only for pages requiring per-request data — auth-gated dashboards, personalized feeds. ISR (`revalidate: N`) hits the sweet spot for content that changes on a schedule: marketing pages, docs, leaderboards. Never use ISR for user-specific data — the cache is shared. For data fetching, Next.js 15 deduplicates `fetch` calls across the same render tree, but parallel fetching still matters. Inside `page.tsx` or a Server Component, fire independent `fetch` calls concurrently with `Promise.all` rather than awaiting them sequentially. Sequential awaits in a layout + page chain compound latency — each segment waits for the previous. Co-locate data fetching in the leaf component that needs it; React's request deduplication prevents over-fetching even when multiple components call the same endpoint.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — route loaders fetch before render, eliminate waterfall, no fetch inside components
bytes.title: Next.js Data Fetching — Hoist Fetches to Server Components, Kill the Waterfall
bytes.body:
Fetching data inside client components creates request waterfalls: the component renders, mounts, then fires a fetch. By the time the response arrives, the user has been staring at a spinner. Next.js App Router eliminates this with async Server Components — fetch runs on the server before any HTML reaches the client.

Make components async, await fetch directly at the top level, and pass data down as props. For parallel independent fetches, use Promise.all — Next.js deduplicates identical fetch calls within a single render pass via its built-in request memoization. For dynamic routes, generateStaticParams pre-renders pages at build time.

Avoid the pattern of fetching in useEffect inside a Client Component unless you explicitly need client-side revalidation (e.g., SWR or React Query for user-specific, real-time data). Even then, seed the initial data via a Server Component and pass it as initialData. Route-level fetches in layout.tsx run in parallel with page.tsx fetches — structure your tree to exploit this.

bytes.topic: Forms — Server Actions, progressive enhancement, form validation on server boundary
bytes.title: Next.js Server Actions — Forms, Progressive Enhancement & Server-Side Validation
bytes.body: Server Actions let you colocate mutation logic with your UI without building a separate API route. Attach one to a `<form>` via the `action` prop and Next.js handles the POST automatically — no `onSubmit`, no `fetch`. The critical detail: because the action runs on the server, validation lives there too. Use Zod inside the action, parse `formData`, and return structured errors. For progressive enhancement, pair this with `useActionState` (React 19 / Next.js 15) — the form works without JS, and when JS loads, you get optimistic UI and inline error display without a full page reload. Never trust client-side validation alone; the server action is the real trust boundary. Avoid revalidating the entire layout on every submission — call `revalidatePath` or `revalidateTag` with surgical precision. Mark the action file with `'use server'` at the top, or inline the directive inside an async function. Inlining inside a Client Component requires the file-level directive on a separate module.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — typed route params, typed loaders, typed search params with Zod
bytes.title: Next.js — Type-Safe Route Params, Search Params, and Loaders with Zod
bytes.body:
Next.js App Router gives you `params` and `searchParams` as plain `unknown` objects — no type safety by default. Zod closes that gap without any extra library overhead.

Define a schema per route segment. In a `[slug]/page.tsx`, parse `params` with `z.object({ slug: z.string() })`. For search params like `?page=2&sort=asc`, parse the `searchParams` prop the same way — coerce numeric strings with `z.coerce.number()`.

For Server Actions and Route Handlers acting as loaders, call `.parse()` at the top of the function. This throws a `ZodError` on bad input, which you catch in a `try/catch` or let Next.js bubble to the nearest `error.tsx`.

Pair this with `generateStaticParams` — type the return value against your Zod schema's inferred type to guarantee build-time and runtime shapes match. No more `params.slug as string` casts scattered across components.

This pattern gives you a single source of truth: the schema drives your TypeScript types, your runtime validation, and your error boundaries — all inside Next.js conventions.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error.tsx / +error.svelte per route segment, notFound(), redirect()
bytes.title: Next.js Error Boundaries — error.tsx, notFound(), and redirect() Per Route Segment
bytes.body:
Next.js App Router scopes error handling to individual route segments via `error.tsx`. Drop one alongside a `page.tsx` and it becomes a React error boundary for that subtree only — sibling segments stay interactive. The component must be a Client Component (`'use client'`) and receives `error` and `reset` props; `reset()` re-renders the segment without a full page reload.

For data-layer errors, `notFound()` throws a special signal that Next.js catches and renders the nearest `not-found.tsx` — skip manual 404 responses entirely. `redirect()` from `next/navigation` works in Server Components, Route Handlers, and Server Actions; it throws internally, so wrap it outside try/catch blocks. In a Server Action, call `redirect()` after awaiting your mutation — Next.js handles the response automatically.

Global fallback lives in `app/error.tsx`; root-level crashes (layout errors) require `app/global-error.tsx`, which replaces the entire `<html>` shell since the root layout is compromised.

bytes.topic: Testing — test loaders and actions in isolation, Playwright for full route flows
bytes.title: Next.js Testing — Isolate Server Actions and Route Handlers, Then Seal It with Playwright
bytes.body:
Server Actions and Route Handlers in the App Router are plain async functions — import and call them directly in Vitest without spinning up a server. For Server Actions, pass a real `FormData` instance. For Route Handlers, construct a `NextRequest` with `new NextRequest('http://localhost/api/posts', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(payload) })` and invoke the exported `POST` function directly. The critical mocks are `next/headers` (`cookies`, `headers`) and `next/navigation` (`redirect`, `notFound`) — these are the trust boundaries where Next.js internals bleed into your logic. Mock them with `vi.mock` before any test imports your module.

Playwright covers what unit tests cannot: RSC streaming, middleware execution, client-side navigation after a Server Action redirect, and Suspense boundary resolution. Point Playwright at `next start` (production build), not `next dev`, to catch `generateStaticParams` misses and build-time errors. Use `test.use({ storageState: 'playwright/.auth/user.json' })` to inject Clerk session cookies for authenticated flows, generated once via `page.context().storageState()` in a global setup file. Assert on `page.url()` after form submission to validate redirect correctness end-to-end.
bytes.code_snippet:
// __tests__/actions/create-post.test.ts
vi.mock('next/headers', () => ({ cookies: vi.fn(() => ({ get: vi.fn() })) }))
vi.mock('next/navigation', () => ({ redirect: vi.fn() }))
vi.mock('@/lib/db', () => ({ db: { post: { create: vi.fn() } } }))

import { createPost } from '@/app/actions/create-post'
import { db } from '@/lib/db'
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'

it('persists post and redirects to slug', async () => {
  vi.mocked(db.post.create).mockResolvedValue({ slug: 'hello-nextjs' })
  const fd = new FormData()
  fd.set('title', 'Hello Next.js')
  await createPost(fd)
  expect(db.post.create).toHaveBeenCalledWith({ data: { title: 'Hello Next.js' } })
  expect(redirect).toHaveBeenCalledWith('/posts/hello-nextjs')
})

bytes.topic: Security — Server Actions as trust boundary, validate all input, env var separation
bytes.title: Next.js Server Actions Are Not Auto-Secure — Treat Them as Public API Endpoints
bytes.body:
Server Actions feel internal, but every `'use server'` function is an exposed HTTP endpoint Next.js generates under `/_next/action`. Any client — including curl — can invoke them directly. Never assume the caller is your own UI.

Validate every input with Zod before touching the database or running business logic. Don't rely on client-side validation as a gate. A hidden form field or a raw fetch to the action URL bypasses your React form entirely.

Env var separation is equally critical. Variables prefixed `NEXT_PUBLIC_` are bundled into the client — never put secrets there. Database URLs, API keys, and signing secrets must be unprefixed and only accessed inside Server Actions or Route Handlers. Accidentally referencing `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_*` in an action is fine, but the reverse — a secret leaking into `NEXT_PUBLIC_*` — ships your key to every browser.

Also enforce authorization explicitly: `auth()` from Clerk (or your session provider) must be called at the top of every action. There is no framework-level guard — unauthenticated invocations reach your action body unless you stop them.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — route segment conventions, consistent loader/action naming
bytes.title: Next.js Route Segment Conventions — Naming Loaders and Actions for Maintainable Apps
bytes.body:
Next.js App Router enforces file-based conventions (`page.tsx`, `layout.tsx`, `loading.tsx`, `error.tsx`), but inside those files naming discipline is entirely on you. Without consistent patterns, async data functions scatter into unmaintainable chaos across large route trees.

Adopt a strict convention: prefix Server Component data functions with `load` and Server Actions with `action`. For a route at `app/dashboard/posts/[id]/`, your `page.tsx` exports a default component that calls `loadPost(id)` — a local async function co-located in the same file or a sibling `loader.ts`. Mutations live in `actions.ts` alongside, exporting named functions like `actionUpdatePost` and `actionDeletePost`.

This mirrors the loader/action mental model from Remix but fits App Router's file conventions. Co-locating `loader.ts` and `actions.ts` per route segment keeps feature logic self-contained, makes parallel route refactors surgical (move the folder, done), and gives you instant grep-ability — `actionUpdate` always means a Server Action, never ambiguous. Pair with TypeScript's `use server` directive at the top of `actions.ts` to enforce the boundary.

bytes.topic: Routing — file-based routing patterns, dynamic segments, catch-all, intercepting routes, parallel routes
bytes.title: Next.js Routing — Dynamic Segments, Catch-All, Parallel & Intercepting Routes
bytes.body: Next.js App Router maps the filesystem directly to URL segments. A folder named `[id]` creates a dynamic segment; `[[...slug]]` creates an optional catch-all that also matches the root. Parallel routes use named slots — `@modal` and `@feed` inside a layout both render simultaneously in the same viewport, each managing their own loading and error boundaries independently. This is how you build dashboard UIs with co-dependent panels without lifting state. Intercepting routes use the `(.)`, `(..)`, or `(...)` conventions to render a route in the current context while keeping the original URL intact — perfect for modal photo galleries that deep-link. The combination is powerful: a `@modal` slot with an intercepting route like `(..)photo/[id]` lets you open a photo in a modal from a feed, while direct navigation to `/photo/[id]` renders the full page. Layouts share state across both paths, so the background feed never unmounts.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — i18n routing strategies, locale in URL vs cookie, server-side locale detection
bytes.title: Next.js i18n — URL-Based Routing vs Cookie Locale, and Server-Side Detection
bytes.body:
Next.js App Router handles i18n through middleware-driven URL prefixes — there's no built-in `i18n` config like Pages Router had. The recommended pattern: store locale in the URL (`/en/dashboard`, `/fr/dashboard`), detect it server-side in middleware, and redirect accordingly. This makes locale cacheable at the CDN level and shareable via URL.

Cookie-based locale skips the URL prefix — useful for apps where clean URLs matter — but breaks CDN caching since locale is invisible to the edge. You'd read `cookies().get('locale')` in a Server Component or middleware and render the appropriate translations without redirecting.

Server-side detection in middleware uses the `Accept-Language` header via `negotiator` + `@formatjs/intl-localematcher`. Match against your supported locales, fall back to default, then `NextResponse.redirect()` to the prefixed path. Use `matcher` to exclude static assets and API routes. For the App Router, locale lives in `params.lang` from a `[lang]` dynamic segment — pass it to your `getDictionary(lang)` server-side loader, never expose translation bundles to the client unnecessarily.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — image optimization with framework Image component, font loading, LCP via priority flag
bytes.title: Next.js Web Vitals — Nail LCP with Image Optimization, Font Loading, and the priority Flag
bytes.body: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) is almost always your hero image or above-the-fold text. Next.js gives you exact tools to fix both. For images, swap `` for `next/image`. It auto-generates `srcset`, serves WebP/AVIF via the built-in Image Optimization API, and lazy-loads by default. The critical addition: `priority` prop on your above-the-fold image. This injects a `` in the ``, eliminating the waterfall delay that tanks LCP scores. For fonts, use `next/font` — never load Google Fonts via a `` tag. `next/font/google` downloads at build time, self-hosts on your domain, and injects `font-display: swap` automatically. Zero layout shift, zero third-party DNS lookup penalty. Combine both: `priority` on `` ensures the browser fetches your hero before parsing the rest of the document; `next/font` ensures text renders immediately with the fallback, swapping when the font is ready. Together they routinely push LCP under the 2.5s Good threshold without any manual preload juggling.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — streaming with Suspense, abort signals in loaders, cleanup on navigation
bytes.title: Next.js Memory Leaks — Suspense Streaming, Abort Signals, and Navigation Cleanup
bytes.body:
Three leak vectors bite Next.js apps in production. First, streaming Server Components: when Suspense boundaries flush chunks and the user navigates away, uncancelled upstream fetches keep running server-side. Inside Route Handlers, forward `request.signal` directly into every downstream `fetch()` — Next.js marks it aborted the moment the client disconnects, collapsing the entire call chain at zero cost.

Second, Client Components doing data fetching with `useEffect`: without an `AbortController`, the promise resolves and calls `setState` on an unmounted tree. In App Router, `<Link>` and `router.push()` unmount the previous page immediately, so this fires on every navigation. Attach `signal: ac.signal` to the fetch and return `() => ac.abort()` from the effect — that's the cleanup hook Next.js triggers on route change.

Third, anything outside React's lifecycle — `setInterval`, WebSocket connections, EventSource for server-sent events — won't auto-cancel. Store them in a `useRef` and tear them down in the same `useEffect` return. React's `use()` hook inside Suspense boundaries propagates abort correctly only when the underlying `fetch()` is wired with a signal in the first place.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — server-sent events from route handlers, combining with client state
bytes.title: Next.js SSE — Streaming Server Events from Route Handlers into Client State
bytes.body:
Next.js Route Handlers support Server-Sent Events natively via the Web Streams API. Return a `ReadableStream` with `Content-Type: text/event-stream` and Next.js will flush chunks as they arrive — no third-party library needed.

On the server, a `ReadableStream` wraps a controller that calls `enqueue()` for each event and `close()` when done. The `cache: 'no-store'` header prevents Next.js from buffering the response.

On the client, `EventSource` connects to the route and fires `onmessage` for each chunk. Wire this into `useState` inside a `useEffect` cleanup pattern: open the source on mount, push each parsed event into state, and call `source.close()` on unmount to avoid memory leaks.

Important gotcha: Next.js Edge Runtime handles SSE better than Node.js runtime for long-lived connections — add `export const runtime = 'edge'` to your route. For App Router, `dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` is also required to prevent static caching of the route segment.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — NEXT_PUBLIC_ vs private vars, validate at startup, never expose secrets
bytes.title: Next.js Environment Variables — NEXT_PUBLIC_ Prefix, Private Vars, and Startup Validation
bytes.body:
Next.js splits environment variables at build time based on a single rule: the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix. Any variable prefixed with NEXT_PUBLIC_ gets inlined into the client bundle via static replacement — Webpack literally replaces process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL with its string value. Strip the prefix and the variable stays server-only, accessible in Route Handlers, Server Components, getServerSideProps, and middleware, but undefined in the browser.

This means secrets like DATABASE_URL or STRIPE_SECRET_KEY must never carry the NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix. If they do, they ship to every user's browser in plain text inside _next/static chunks.

The sharp edge: Next.js does not validate required variables by default. A missing NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL silently becomes the string 'undefined' in the bundle. Fix this with a startup validation module — import it at the top of next.config.ts so the build fails fast before bad config reaches production. The @t3-oss/env-nextjs package wraps Zod and handles the NEXT_PUBLIC_ split automatically, erroring at both build time and runtime if variables are absent or malformed.

bytes.topic: API Layer — route handlers for public APIs, Server Actions for mutations, middleware for auth
bytes.title: Next.js API Layer — Route Handlers, Server Actions, and Middleware Done Right
bytes.body: Next.js gives you three distinct primitives for your API layer, and mixing them up kills both DX and performance. Route Handlers (`app/api/*/route.ts`) are for public-facing REST endpoints — third-party webhooks, mobile clients, or any consumer outside your Next.js app. Export named HTTP methods (`GET`, `POST`) and return a `NextResponse`. Server Actions are for mutations triggered from your own UI. Decorated with `'use server'`, they run on the server, integrate with `useFormState`/`useTransition`, and automatically handle CSRF — no fetch boilerplate needed. Middleware (`middleware.ts`) runs on the Edge before any route resolves. Use it exclusively for auth checks, geo-redirects, and request rewriting — not for heavy data fetching. A common pattern: Middleware validates the Clerk JWT and sets a header; Server Actions read that header via `headers()` from `next/headers`; Route Handlers expose webhook endpoints consumed by Stripe or GitHub. Each primitive has a clear lane — crossing them introduces unnecessary latency or security gaps.

bytes.topic: Animation — page transition patterns, View Transitions API, layout persistence across navigations
bytes.title: Next.js Page Transitions — View Transitions API and Layout Persistence in App Router
bytes.body:
Next.js 15 (App Router) pairs cleanly with the View Transitions API. Enable it via `experimental.viewTransition: true` in `next.config.ts`, then wrap animated subtrees with React 19's `unstable_ViewTransition`. On each `<Link>`-driven navigation, the browser captures before/after snapshots and cross-fades — or you supply custom `::view-transition-*` keyframes in global CSS.

Layout persistence is the real win here. Shared segments (`app/layout.tsx`) never remount across sibling routes, so nav bars, sidebars, and React context stay alive. Only the changed `page.tsx` swaps out. Assign a stable `view-transition-name` via inline style to any element that should morph rather than fade — the browser matches it by name across the transition snapshot pair.

For per-element control, `useViewTransitionState(href)` from `next/navigation` (canary) returns `isTransitioning`, letting you drive CSS class changes precisely when a transition to that href is active — useful for animating active link indicators or card expansions tied to route changes.

One hard constraint: `startViewTransition` is single-document only. `router.refresh()` calls and any navigation that triggers a full page reload skip the animation silently.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — layout-level toast/modal, shared loading UI in layout segment
bytes.title: Next.js Global UI Patterns — Layout-Level Toast, Modal, and Shared Loading UI
bytes.body:
Next.js App Router's layout segments are the natural home for global UI primitives. Place your ToastProvider and ModalProvider in app/layout.tsx once — every page inherits them without prop drilling. For scoped providers (e.g., dashboard-only toasts), use a route group layout like app/(dashboard)/layout.tsx to avoid polluting the root shell.

Loading states are more idiomatic still: colocate a loading.tsx next to any page.tsx and Next.js automatically wraps that segment in a <Suspense> boundary — no manual wrapping needed. The loading UI streams to the client instantly during navigation while the page's async Server Component fetches, then React flushes the real content when ready.

For modals, the @modal parallel route slot lets you render a modal alongside a page — the URL changes, the background page stays mounted, and closing it calls router.back(). This beats a global useState because modal state survives hard refreshes and is deep-linkable. Wire the slot into your segment layout alongside children and the three patterns compose cleanly into a navigation-aware UI shell — with zero extra client JS for the shell itself.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — automatic code splitting per route, dynamic imports, bundle analysis
bytes.title: Next.js Bundle Optimization — Code Splitting, Dynamic Imports, and Analyzing What Ships
bytes.body:
Next.js automatically code-splits at the route level — each file in `app/` or `pages/` becomes its own JS chunk, so users only download what the current route needs. But route-level splitting isn't enough for heavy third-party libs or rarely-used UI components.

Use `next/dynamic` to lazy-load components with an optional loading skeleton. Pass `ssr: false` for browser-only dependencies like chart libraries or rich text editors that touch `window` directly — this prevents SSR hydration errors and removes them from the server bundle entirely.

To inspect what you're actually shipping, add `@next/bundle-analyzer` to your config. Set `ANALYZE=true` before your build and it opens an interactive treemap showing chunk sizes, shared chunks (the `_app` bundle), and which modules are duplicated across routes.

Watch for: `moment.js` locale bloat (swap for `date-fns`), lodash full-bundle imports (use `lodash-es` with tree-shaking), and large icon sets imported as a barrel (`import * from 'lucide-react'` pulls everything — import individually instead).

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — framework CLI, preview deployments, type-check route params
bytes.title: nextjs Typed Routes — Catch Broken Links and Bad Params at Build Time
bytes.body:
Enable `experimental.typedRoutes: true` in `next.config.ts` and Next.js generates a `__next_route_internal_types__` declaration that maps every file in `app/` to a strongly-typed `Route` union. Once active, `Link href`, `router.push()`, and `redirect()` all accept only valid routes — typos become compile errors, not runtime 404s.

Route segment params are typed via the `Props` convention in App Router. Since Next.js 15, `params` is a `Promise<{ slug: string }>` — you must `await` it. Skipping the `await` causes a runtime error that `tsc` catches before deployment.

Wire this into your quality gate: `next build` runs `tsc` internally, but add `"typecheck": "tsc --noEmit"` to `package.json` scripts for faster CI feedback ahead of the build step. On Vercel, preview deployments run the full build pipeline — a type error on a feature branch blocks the preview URL and surfaces the failure directly in the PR check before any reviewer sees it.

`generateStaticParams` return types are inferred and checked against your `params` shape automatically, so mismatched segment names fail at compile time too.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — edge function logs, server-side Sentry, Web Vitals from RUM
bytes.title: Next.js Production Monitoring — Edge Logs, Server Sentry, and Web Vitals RUM
bytes.body:
Next.js exposes three distinct production monitoring surfaces, each requiring a different approach.

**Edge Runtime logs:** `console.log` in Middleware or Edge API routes streams to Vercel's runtime logs but isn't persisted. Use `waitUntil(promise)` from `next/server` to fire-and-forget to a log drain without blocking response time. Keep logging SDKs out — Edge bundles have a 1 MB hard limit.

**Server-side Sentry:** Initialize via `instrumentation.ts` at the project root. Its `register()` function runs once per cold start across both Node.js and Edge runtimes — branch on `process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME` to avoid loading the Node SDK into the Edge runtime. Wrap Server Actions with `Sentry.withServerActionInstrumentation` and call `captureException` inside `error.tsx` for boundary-caught errors.

**Web Vitals RUM:** The App Router's `useReportWebVitals` hook (imported from `next/web-vitals`) fires on LCP, CLS, INP, FCP, and TTFB. Drop it into a `'use client'` component in your root layout and pipe to `/api/vitals` or `Sentry.metrics.distribution` for percentile bucketing.

Critical gotcha: `instrumentation.ts` must live at the project root (or `src/` root) — not inside `app/`.

bytes.topic: Middleware — run at the edge before render, auth guards, A/B testing, geolocation, rate limiting
bytes.title: Next.js Middleware — Edge Auth, A/B Testing, and Geo Routing Before First Byte
bytes.body:
Next.js Middleware runs on the V8-based Edge Runtime — not Node.js — meaning no `fs`, no `net`, but sub-millisecond cold starts globally. Drop a `middleware.ts` at the project root and it intercepts every matched request before rendering begins.

The `matcher` config in `export const config` controls scope precisely — `['/dashboard/:path*']` keeps you from running auth logic on every static asset. Inside the function, `NextResponse.redirect()`, `.rewrite()`, and `.next()` are your three tools: redirect sends the user elsewhere, rewrite silently swaps the underlying route, and next forwards the request with optional header mutations.

For auth guards, read from `req.cookies` and call a lightweight JWT verify — no DB round-trips, keep it Edge-compatible. For A/B tests, read or set a bucketing cookie and pass the bucket via a request header (`x-ab-bucket`) so Server Components can read it via `headers()`. For geolocation, `req.geo?.country` is populated automatically on Vercel's edge network. Rate limiting requires an external store — use `@upstash/ratelimit`, which ships an Edge-compatible client.
